article_title: Gmail’s long-awaited email address change has finally arrived
core_thesis: Google has finally rolled out the ability for Gmail users in the United States to change their primary email address (username), a long-requested feature that was tested in December 2025 and formally announced in March 2026. The article details the steps to change the address, potential issues (especially for Chromebook users), and limitations such as a 12-month cooldown before creating a new Gmail address and the inability to delete the new address.
detailed_summary: Google announced that Gmail users in the United States can now change their primary email address via account settings (Personal info > Email > Google Account email > Change Google Account email). The feature was tested in December 2025 and is now rolling out. Users can also add a third-party email as an alternative login method or delete their Gmail entirely. Important caveats include: Chromebook users may need to remove their local account before changing and re-sign in, which could affect Android/Linux apps; the old email is retained as an alternative address (can be deleted manually); users can revert to the previous address at any time but cannot create a new Gmail address for 12 months; the new address cannot be deleted. The feature is currently US-only, with no timeline for other regions. The article also mentions a related update: Google is expanding Personal Intelligence (a Gemini feature) to free accounts.

entities.name: Google
entities.role: Company that owns Gmail and rolled out the email address change feature.

entities.name: Corbin Davenport
entities.role: Author of the article, former News Editor at How-To Geek.

entities.name: How-To Geek
entities.role: Technology news website that published the article.

key_data_points: Google is rolling out the ability to change your primary Gmail address (username) for US accounts, announced March 31, 2026. (🟢 Relevant to you as a user: if you have an old Gmail address you want to update, this is now possible.)

To change your address: visit Google account email settings, click 'Personal info', then Email > Google Account email > Change Google Account email. (🟢 Practical step-by-step for anyone wanting to change their Gmail username.)

Chromebook users must remove their local account before changing and re-sign in after the switch, otherwise the home directory may appear empty. This can affect Android and Linux apps that are not easily backed up. (🟢 Important for homelab/DevOps users who rely on Chromebooks for development or testing.)

After changing, the old email is saved as an alternative address and you will still receive emails there. You can delete the old address manually if desired. (🟢 Useful for managing multiple identities or migrating accounts.)

You can change back to your previous email at any time, but you cannot create a new Gmail address for 12 months, and you cannot delete the new address. (🟢 Important limitation to consider before switching.)
The feature is currently US-only; no timeline for other regions. (🟢 If you are outside the US, you may need to wait or use a VPN to access the feature.)
methodology_steps: 1. Go to your Google Account settings (myaccount.google.com).
2. Click on 'Personal info' in the left sidebar.
4. Next to 'Google Account email', click 'Change Google Account email'.
5. Follow the prompts to enter your new desired email address and confirm the change.
6. If you use a Chromebook, before changing, remove your local account (Settings > Accounts > Remove this account). After the change, sign in again with the new email to update the home directory.
7. Optionally, delete the old email address from your account settings if you no longer want it as an alternative.
conclusions: Google has finally delivered a long-requested feature: the ability to change your primary Gmail address. This is a significant quality-of-life improvement for users who created accounts with outdated or undesirable usernames.

The feature comes with important caveats: Chromebook users face extra steps, and there is a 12-month cooldown on creating new Gmail addresses. Users should plan accordingly, especially if they rely on multiple Google services.
The rollout is US-only for now, but given the demand, it is likely to expand globally. Users in other regions may consider using a VPN to access the feature, though this is not officially supported.
personal_relevance_summary: As a student and programmer, you may have multiple Google accounts for different purposes (personal, academic, projects). This feature allows you to consolidate or rename accounts without losing data. For homelab/DevOps users, the Chromebook caveat is critical if you use a Chromebook for development. The ability to change your primary email can also help with account management for cybersecurity labs or testing environments. Additionally, if you are a Red Teamer, be aware that email address changes could be used in social engineering or account recovery attacks—understanding the process helps you assess risks.
extra_advice: If you are outside the US and want to use this feature now, consider using a VPN with a US endpoint. However, be aware that Google may detect VPN usage and block the change. Also, if you manage multiple Google accounts for homelab or testing, plan the migration carefully to avoid losing access to services like Google Cloud or Firebase.
